albums.title: Discovery
albums.reason: With Digital Love, One More Time and Superheroes all in your all-time top tracks, Discovery is the album you keep returning to without realising it — worth a full front-to-back run.

albums.title: In Rainbows
albums.reason: Reckoner is your third most-played track of all time, and it lives here — In Rainbows is the Radiohead record that rewards slow, attentive listening you may not have given it in a while.

albums.title: The Dark Side of the Moon
albums.reason: Pink Floyd sits at nearly 1,200 all-time plays for you — this is the cornerstone album, and it has been long enough since most people's last full listen to feel completely fresh again.

albums.title: Moon Safari
albums.reason: Air is one of your top 10 all-time artists with over 1,000 plays — Moon Safari is their definitive statement and the kind of record that feels like Sunday afternoon comfort.

albums.title: Melody A.M.
albums.reason: Röyksopp is your fifth most-played artist of all time — Melody A.M. is their career peak, and its slow-burning warmth is exactly the vault comfort this tier is built for.

albums.title: Leftism
albums.reason: Release the Pressure is your fourth most-played track ever, and it opens Leftism — one of the great debut albums, built for big speakers and full attention.

albums.title: James Blake
albums.reason: Postpone is your most-played track of all time and CMYK is close behind — both live on this debut, which you clearly love deeply but may not have sat with as a whole recently.

albums.title: In Sides
albums.reason: Orbital is a firm fixture in your all-time top 20 — In Sides is their most ambitious and transportive record, and it has the kind of depth that rewards a return visit.

albums.title: II
albums.reason: You love Apparat and the intersection of techno and emotional depth that defines your all-time taste — Moderat, which includes Apparat, makes records that sit exactly between your love of Radiohead and Leftfield.

albums.title: Music Has the Right to Children
albums.reason: Given your deep love of Daft Punk, Röyksopp and Air, this Scottish duo's dusty, warm, slightly eerie electronic world is the cult classic you have almost certainly been orbiting without landing on.

albums.title: Mezzanine
albums.reason: The dark, atmospheric side of your all-time taste — Radiohead, Underworld, James Blake — points directly at Mezzanine, Massive Attack's most gothic and hypnotic record.

albums.title: The Last Resort
albums.reason: If Leftfield's tension, Justice's edge and Orbital's architecture all appeal to you, Trentemøller's debut is the Danish cult record that ties those threads together with devastating atmospherics.

albums.title: Swim
albums.reason: Your love of Daft Punk's melodic side, Röyksopp and Air suggests you'd be drawn to Caribou — Swim is his most immersive record, sitting between krautrock, psychedelia and club music.

albums.title: Untrue
albums.reason: James Blake has cited Burial as a core influence, and Untrue is the record that defines late-night fragmented bass music — given how deeply you connect with Blake, this is essential listening.

albums.title: v2.0
albums.reason: The Oscar Peterson Trio plays you've logged recently sit alongside your all-time love of electronic music — GoGo Penguin bridges that exact gap, playing acoustic jazz with the dynamics and energy of IDM.

albums.title: Immunity
albums.reason: You have deep roots in Daft Punk, Orbital and Leftfield — Immunity is the modern record that honours that lineage while pushing it into something more emotionally raw and physically overwhelming.

albums.title: Portico Quartet
albums.reason: Your recent listening arc — Hania Rani, Nils Frahm, Brambles, Ólafur Arnalds — points to a love of contemplative, textured instrumental music, and Portico Quartet sit precisely in that space with a jazz-rooted twist.

albums.title: Elaenia
albums.reason: Your recent Four Tet and DJ Koze plays suggest you're drawn to electronic music with a beating, organic heart — Elaenia is Floating Points' debut and one of the most beautiful records in that territory.

albums.title: At the Pershing: But Not for Me
albums.reason: You have been deep in Oscar Peterson Trio and Bill Evans this year — Ahmad Jamal is the pianist who influenced Miles Davis and defined a lighter, space-conscious style that sits perfectly alongside those artists.

albums.title: Rose in the Dark
albums.reason: Your recent plays of Bill Withers, Charles Bradley and Sault reveal a love of warm, deeply soulful music — Cleo Sol, who records with Sault's team, makes records drenched in that same unhurried feeling.

albums.title: All Is Wild, All Is Silent
albums.reason: Brambles' Charcoal and Hania Rani's Ghosts are both in your recent heavy rotation — Balmorhea make slow, aching chamber music built for the same kind of quiet attention.

albums.title: Journey in Satchidananda
albums.reason: Your recent Oscar Peterson Trio and Bill Evans listening combined with your ambient and electronic interests suggests Alice Coltrane's devotional harp-led jazz would resonate deeply — this album is a singular experience.

albums.title: The Universe Smiling Upon You
albums.reason: Your recent diet of Bill Withers soul, 9 Lazy 9 downtempo and DJ Koze's warmth points toward Khruangbin — a Texas trio making deeply relaxed, globally-flavoured music that sits in that same slow, soulful lane.

albums.title: Black Focus
albums.reason: You have been deep in Oscar Peterson, Bill Withers soul and the organic side of electronic music — Black Focus fuses jazz drumming, Afrobeat rhythm and electric keys into something that captures all of that at once.

albums.title: Solipsism
albums.reason: Joep Beving is your most-played artist of the last seven days by a wide margin — Solipsism is his debut and the record that started everything, and it is not on your recently-heard list.

albums.title: Waltz for Debby
albums.reason: Bill Evans has been a constant presence this month — Waltz for Debby is his most beloved live record and it has a gentle, conversational warmth that suits exactly where your listening is right now.

albums.title: Geography
albums.reason: You have been listening to Tom Misch this month and Geography — his debut album — is not on your recent list, making it the natural next step from wherever Full Circle left you.

albums.title: Smokers Delight
albums.reason: Nightmares on Wax has been part of your recent listening and Smokers Delight is their classic — a slow, heavy, deeply satisfying record that pairs perfectly with the jazz mood you are in right now.

albums.title: Little Ghost
albums.reason: Moonchild's Voyager has been in your recent rotation — Little Ghost is their debut, and its intimate, jazz-drenched neo-soul feel sits perfectly alongside the Bill Evans and Tom Misch energy of this week.

albums.title: Solo
albums.reason: Nils Frahm has been present across both this week and this month, and Solo — recorded in a single night on a historic Bösendorfer — is not on your recently-heard list and captures exactly the quiet, reflective mood you are in.

albums.title: South Wind, Clear Sky (Part 2)
albums.reason: scruscru is one of your most-played artists this week with tracks from the South Wind series in your recent history — Part 2 is the natural companion and keeps that warm, organic jazz-house feeling going.

albums.title: The Chronicles of a Burning Heart
albums.reason: Folamour appeared in your recent tracks this week — this album is his most emotionally direct statement, full of the same gospel-tinged, soul-saturated house that you have been gravitating toward lately.

albums.title: Tides
albums.reason: Felix Rösch has been part of your listening this month and Berceuse from his Piano record has been a recent track for you — Tides is a different release that extends that same hushed, beautiful solo piano world.

songs.title: Instant Crush
songs.reason: You have an enormous number of Daft Punk plays across multiple tracks — Instant Crush from Random Access Memories is one of their most affecting songs and may not have had a spin in a while.

songs.title: How to Disappear Completely
songs.reason: Reckoner is your third most-played track of all time, and if that song moves you, How to Disappear Completely is its spiritual counterpart — arguably Radiohead's most devastating piece of music.

songs.title: Remind Me
songs.reason: Röyksopp sits in your all-time top five artists — Remind Me is one of their signature tracks, a perfect piece of melancholic electronic pop that feels like a postcard from a better day.

songs.title: Cowgirl
songs.reason: Born Slippy is in your all-time top tracks and Underworld is a core artist for you — Cowgirl is the deeper cult pick from Dubnobasswithmyheadman, a 10-minute transcendence that rewards being revisited.

songs.title: D.A.N.C.E.
songs.reason: Justice sits in your all-time top 20 artists — D.A.N.C.E. is their most joyful, widely-loved track and one of those songs that is impossible to listen to without feeling something lift.

songs.title: Last Nite
songs.reason: The Strokes are a fixture in your all-time top 20 — Last Nite is one of those songs that hits differently when you haven't heard it in months, all nervous energy and perfectly worn-in cool.

songs.title: Comfortably Numb
songs.reason: Pink Floyd is a deep all-time artist for you — Comfortably Numb is one of those tracks that no amount of familiarity can diminish, especially that second guitar solo, which remains extraordinary.

songs.title: Runaway
songs.reason: Kanye West sits in your all-time top 20 — Runaway is his career-defining statement, a nine-minute meditation on ego and regret that rewards being listened to in full with headphones.

songs.title: Moan
songs.reason: If Leftfield's Release the Pressure is one of your most-played tracks, you will understand exactly what Moan is doing — it builds the same slow pressure and releases it with the same inevitability.

songs.title: Archangel
songs.reason: James Blake's CMYK and Postpone dominate your all-time chart — Archangel is the Burial track that most directly inspired that sound, and it is one of the most quietly devastating pieces of British music ever made.

songs.title: Kong
songs.reason: You liked Bonobo's Kiara as a positive signal — Kong from Black Sands is its more propulsive, rhythmically intense counterpart, showing the full range of what Bonobo does at his most energised.

songs.title: Open Eye Signal
songs.reason: Your deep runs with Daft Punk, Orbital and Leftfield show you understand what a truly great electronic track feels like when it builds — Open Eye Signal is one of the finest examples of the last 20 years.

songs.title: Sun
songs.reason: The melodic warmth of your Daft Punk plays and Air listens points to Caribou — Sun is the track that captures everything intoxicating about his sound in under five minutes.

songs.title: Roygbiv
songs.reason: If you love the dreamlike quality of Air and Röyksopp, Roygbiv is a two-minute piece of electronic music that feels like a memory you can't quite place — small, perfect, and completely unforgettable.

songs.title: Hopopono
songs.reason: Your Oscar Peterson Trio plays alongside your all-time electronic listening suggests GoGo Penguin would click immediately — Hopopono is the track that usually does it, with its rapid-fire piano lines and propulsive rhythm.

songs.title: Bad Kingdom
songs.reason: Your love of Radiohead's darker moods and Apparat's A Hum Of Maybe makes Bad Kingdom the natural next discovery — it has that same quality of something beautiful being held just slightly out of reach.

songs.title: LesAlpx
songs.reason: Your recent Four Tet and Nils Frahm plays show you are drawn to music that is both electronic and deeply human in texture — LesAlpx is Floating Points at his most meditative and is a natural entry point.

songs.title: Poinciana
songs.reason: You have spent serious time with Oscar Peterson this year — Poinciana is one of the most famous piano jazz recordings ever made, and Ahmad Jamal's approach to space and silence will feel immediately right to you.

songs.title: Golden
songs.reason: Your recent Bill Withers and Charles Bradley plays reveal a love of deeply soulful, unhurried music — Golden is Cleo Sol at her most luminous, and it has that same quality of feeling completely timeless.

songs.title: A Calf Born in Winter
songs.reason: The combination of Bill Withers soul and 9 Lazy 9 downtempo in your recent listening is essentially the blueprint for this track — unhurried, warm and built around a groove that just keeps rolling.

songs.title: Calligraphy
songs.reason: Your recent Oscar Peterson Trio and Sault listening shows you are in a deeply soulful, rhythmically alive place — Calligraphy is the track that proves Yussef Kamaal are doing something genuinely special.

songs.title: Clipper
songs.reason: Hania Rani's Ghosts and Brambles' Charcoal are two of your most-played recent albums — Clipper has that same quality of suspension and stillness, but with a jazz ensemble doing the hovering.

songs.title: Something About John Coltrane
songs.reason: Your recent Oscar Peterson Trio and ambient listening arc points toward Alice Coltrane as a natural discovery — this track captures the hypnotic, spiritual side of her work that makes her sound unlike anyone else.

songs.title: Remembrance
songs.reason: Brambles' Such Owls as You and Hania Rani's The Boat are two of your most-played recent tracks — Remembrance occupies that same quiet, aching space and would sit perfectly between them.

songs.title: Sleeping Lotus
songs.reason: Joep Beving is dominating your week — Sleeping Lotus is one of his most loved individual tracks and it captures the hushed, inward-looking mood that seems to be exactly where you are right now.

songs.title: Peace Piece
songs.reason: Bill Evans has been a constant presence this month — Peace Piece is a seven-minute solo improvisation of almost hypnotic stillness, and it maps perfectly onto the contemplative piano mood you are clearly in.

songs.title: It Runs Through Me
songs.reason: Tom Misch has been in your recent rotation — It Runs Through Me, featuring De La Soul, is one of his most loved tracks and captures the easy, sun-warmed groove that fits this week's mood perfectly.

songs.title: The List
songs.reason: Moonchild's Voyager has been part of your recent listening — The List from their debut is a quietly devastating track that sits in the same intimate, jazz-tinged soul space you have been inhabiting.

songs.title: Pulpulak
songs.reason: Pulpulak appeared in your most recent tracks this week — flagging it here as a standout worth a conscious, focused listen rather than letting it pass by in the background.

songs.title: A Lovely Valentine for My Haters
songs.reason: Folamour has been in your recent listening this week — this track from Chronicles of a Burning Heart is his most emotionally open moment, full of the gospel warmth and ecstatic energy that defines his best work.

songs.title: Says
songs.reason: Nils Frahm has been present across your week and month — Says is his most iconic piece, a slow accumulation of piano and organ that rewards being played loud through good speakers, which seems like the right move right now.

songs.title: Autumn Leaves
songs.reason: Oscar Peterson has been your most-played artist of the past month — Autumn Leaves is one of the great jazz standards and Peterson's version is among the most joyful and alive, perfect for wherever this week takes you.

djSets.setName: HMWL Podcast 430: Niconé & David Hasert (Kompakt / Get Physical / HMWL)
Daphni - FOLD, London. Nov 22, 2025. 7hr DJ set
Klausmarie b2b Alec Troniq - RAVIN' RAVIN' 2026 @ Sektor Evolution
BENT THE KNEE TO HER VOL. 1
